questions.question:
In which of the cases stated below is the following statement true?
"For every non-deterministic machine M_1 there exists an equivalent deterministic machine M_2 recognizing the same language."
A. M_1 is non-deterministic finite automaton.
B. M_1 is non-deterministic PDA.
C. M_1 is a non-deterministic Turing machine.
D. For no machines M_1 and M_2, the above statement is true.
questions.to_find: In which cases does a non-deterministic machine have an equivalent deterministic machine recognizing the same language?

understand.plain: This question asks about which types of non-deterministic machines can always be simulated by a deterministic machine that accepts exactly the same language. For finite automata (NFA to DFA) and Turing machines (NTM to DTM), such equivalence holds. However, for pushdown automata (NPDA to DPDA), there exist context-free languages that can be recognized by NPDA but not by any DPDA — so option B is false.

keywords.explain: A finite automaton where on a given state and symbol, multiple transitions are possible. It accepts a string if ANY computation path accepts.
keywords.example: An NFA can be in states {q1, q2} simultaneously; equivalent DFA uses subset construction.

keywords.explain: A pushdown automaton where multiple transitions are allowed. Its power is strictly greater than deterministic PDA.
keywords.example: The language {ww^R | w in {a,b}*} requires NPDA but cannot be recognized by any DPDA.

keywords.explain: A Turing machine where multiple transitions are allowed; accepts if ANY branch accepts. Equivalent in language-recognition power to deterministic TM.
keywords.example: NTM and DTM both recognize the same class of recursively enumerable languages.

understand.visual_alt: Hierarchy: NFA = DFA (regular languages); NPDA strictly contains DPDA (context-free vs deterministic context-free); NTM = DTM (recursively enumerable languages).

given.aim: Identify which classes of non-deterministic machines have deterministic equivalents recognizing the same language.

terms.meaning: Every NFA can be converted to an equivalent DFA via the subset construction algorithm.
terms.example: NFA with n states -> DFA with at most 2^n states.

terms.meaning: NPDAs strictly outpower DPDAs; some CFLs (like palindromes) cannot be recognized by any DPDA.
terms.example: {ww^R} is a CFL accepted by NPDA but not by DPDA.

terms.meaning: Every NTM can be simulated by a DTM using breadth-first simulation; both recognize recursively enumerable languages.
terms.example: DTM explores all NTM computation branches; accepts if any branch accepts.

given.plan: Recall the known results: NFA = DFA (subset construction), NPDA strictly stronger than DPDA, NTM = DTM in language recognition. Options A and C are true; B is false; D is false since A and C are true.

steps.title: Check NFA vs DFA
steps.formula_raw: Every NFA can be converted to an equivalent DFA (subset construction)
steps.apply: The subset construction theorem guarantees that for every NFA there exists a DFA recognizing exactly the same regular language. So option A is TRUE.
steps.note: The DFA may have exponentially more states but recognizes the same language.

steps.title: Check NPDA vs DPDA
steps.formula_raw: L(DPDA) subset L(NPDA); L(NPDA) = CFL; L(DPDA) = DCFL (proper subset)
steps.apply: There exist context-free languages (e.g., {ww^R}) accepted by NPDAs but not by any DPDA. The two classes are not equivalent in language recognition. So option B is FALSE.
steps.note: NPDA recognizes all CFLs; DPDA only recognizes DCFLs, a proper subset.

steps.title: Check NTM vs DTM
steps.formula_raw: Every NTM can be simulated by a DTM (BFS over computation tree)
steps.apply: A DTM can simulate an NTM by doing a breadth-first traversal of all non-deterministic computation paths. Both NTM and DTM recognize exactly the class of recursively enumerable languages. So option C is TRUE.
steps.note: This equivalence is only about language recognition, not time/space complexity.

steps.title: Evaluate option D
steps.apply: Since A and C are true, option D (no such machines exist) is FALSE.
steps.note: The correct answers are A and C.

solution.result: A and C — NFA has an equivalent DFA, and NTM has an equivalent DTM.

questions.formula_note: Subset construction (NFA to DFA) and BFS simulation of NTM by DTM; NPDA strictly outpowers DPDA.

questions.question:
Which of the following conversions is not possible (algorithmically)?
A. Regular grammar to context free grammar
B. Non-deterministic FSA to deterministic FSA
C. Non-deterministic PDA to deterministic PDA
D. Non-deterministic Turing machine to deterministic Turing machine
questions.answer: C. Non-deterministic PDA to deterministic PDA
questions.to_find: Which conversion between machine/grammar types is NOT always possible algorithmically?

understand.plain: The question asks which of the listed conversions cannot always be done algorithmically. Regular grammars can always be converted to CFGs (trivially). NFA can always be converted to DFA. NTM can always be converted to DTM. But NPDA cannot always be converted to an equivalent DPDA — there are context-free languages not recognizable by any DPDA.

keywords.explain: A PDA where at each configuration at most one move is possible. Recognizes only deterministic context-free languages (DCFL).
keywords.example: DPDA can recognize {a^n b^n} but not {ww^R}.

keywords.explain: A PDA with multiple possible moves; recognizes all context-free languages.
keywords.example: NPDA can recognize palindromes {ww^R} by guessing the midpoint non-deterministically.

understand.visual_alt: Language class hierarchy: Regular (DFA=NFA) subset DCFL (DPDA) subset CFL (NPDA) subset CSL subset RE (DTM=NTM).

given.aim: Identify which conversion is not always algorithmically possible.

terms.meaning: Any regular grammar is also a context-free grammar (every regular production rule is also a valid CFG rule).
terms.example: A -> aB is both a regular and a context-free production.
terms.connects: Option A is always possible — trivial inclusion.

terms.meaning: Subset construction converts any NFA to an equivalent DFA.
terms.example: NFA with epsilon-transitions -> equivalent DFA via epsilon-closure.

terms.meaning: NPDA recognizes all CFLs; DPDA recognizes only DCFLs. Some CFLs have no equivalent DPDA.
terms.example: {ww^R | w in {a,b}*} is accepted by NPDA but by no DPDA.
terms.connects: Option C is NOT always possible.

terms.meaning: BFS simulation allows any NTM to be converted to a DTM recognizing the same language.
terms.example: DTM simulates NTM by exploring all branches level-by-level.

given.plan: Check each conversion: A (trivial inclusion), B (subset construction), C (NPDA strictly more powerful than DPDA — impossible for some CFLs), D (BFS simulation). The answer is C.

steps.title: Evaluate option A: Regular grammar to CFG
steps.formula_raw: Every regular grammar is a special case of CFG
steps.apply: Regular grammars have productions of form A -> aB or A -> a. These are valid CFG productions. The conversion is trivially possible by keeping the same grammar.
steps.note: The inclusion L(Regular) subset L(CFG) is strict, but the grammar itself can be viewed as a CFG.

steps.title: Evaluate option B: NFA to DFA
steps.formula_raw: Subset construction: DFA states = subsets of NFA states
steps.apply: The well-known subset construction gives an algorithmic procedure to convert any NFA (including with epsilon-transitions) to an equivalent DFA. Always possible.
steps.note: May cause exponential blowup in number of states.

steps.title: Evaluate option C: NPDA to DPDA
steps.apply: DPDA recognizes only deterministic context-free languages. The language {ww^R | w in {a,b}*} is a CFL accepted by an NPDA but cannot be recognized by any DPDA. No algorithm can convert an arbitrary NPDA to a DPDA because equivalent DPDA may not exist.
steps.note: This is the key distinction: for PDAs, non-determinism adds real power.

steps.title: Evaluate option D: NTM to DTM
steps.formula_raw: NTM simulation by DTM via BFS over computation tree
steps.apply: A DTM can simulate an NTM by performing BFS over all computation paths. Both recognize the same class (RE languages). Conversion is always possible.
steps.note: For Turing machines, non-determinism does NOT add recognition power.

solution.result: C. Non-deterministic PDA to deterministic PDA — this conversion is not always possible algorithmically.

questions.formula_note: NPDA strictly outpowers DPDA; L(NPDA) = CFL while L(DPDA) = DCFL (proper subset of CFL).

questions.question:
Regarding the power of recognition of languages, which of the following statements is false?
A. The non-deterministic finite-state automata are equivalent to deterministic finite-state automata.
B. Non-deterministic Push-down automata are equivalent to deterministic Push-down automata.
C. Non-deterministic Turing machines are equivalent to deterministic Turing machines.
D. Multi-tape Turing machines are equivalent to Single-tape Turing machines.
questions.answer: B. Non-deterministic Push-down automata are equivalent to deterministic Push-down automata.
questions.to_find: Which statement about equivalence of machine classes in language recognition power is FALSE?

understand.plain: The question tests knowledge of which machine equivalences hold. NFA = DFA is true (regular languages). NTM = DTM is true (RE languages). Multi-tape TM = single-tape TM is true. But NPDA is strictly more powerful than DPDA — they are NOT equivalent in language recognition power. Statement B is false.

keywords.explain: Two automata classes are equivalent if they recognize exactly the same set of languages.
keywords.example: NFA and DFA both recognize exactly the class of regular languages.

keywords.explain: A TM with multiple tapes and heads. Any multi-tape TM can be simulated by a single-tape TM, so their recognition power is identical.
keywords.example: A 2-tape TM running in time T(n) can be simulated by a 1-tape TM in time O(T(n)^2).

understand.visual_alt: Language class equivalences: NFA = DFA (regular), NTM = DTM = multi-tape TM (RE). NPDA vs DPDA is NOT an equivalence — NPDA is strictly more powerful.

given.aim: Find the false statement among the equivalence claims.

terms.meaning: Both recognize exactly the regular languages; subset construction converts NFA to DFA.
terms.example: NFA for (a|b)*abb -> DFA by subset construction.

terms.meaning: NPDA recognizes all CFLs; DPDA only recognizes DCFLs. They are NOT equivalent.
terms.example: Palindromes over {a,b} form a CFL recognized by NPDA but not by any DPDA.
terms.connects: Statement B is FALSE — this is the answer.

terms.meaning: Both recognize exactly the recursively enumerable languages; NTM adds no extra power in terms of what languages can be recognized.
terms.example: BFS on NTM branches by a DTM recognizes the same language.

terms.meaning: Any multi-tape TM can be simulated by a single-tape TM recognizing the same language.
terms.example: Encode multiple tapes on one tape with special delimiter symbols.

given.plan: Evaluate each equivalence claim. The known false one is B — NPDA and DPDA are NOT equivalent; NPDA is strictly more powerful.

steps.title: Check statement A: NFA equivalent to DFA
steps.formula_raw: Subset construction: L(NFA) = L(DFA) = Regular languages
steps.apply: Subset construction proves every NFA has an equivalent DFA. Statement A is TRUE.
steps.note: This is a classical theorem in automata theory.

steps.title: Check statement B: NPDA equivalent to DPDA
steps.apply: NPDA can recognize palindromes {ww^R} and other non-DCFL languages. No DPDA can recognize these. The two classes are NOT equivalent. Statement B is FALSE.
steps.note: Non-determinism adds genuine expressive power for PDA, unlike for finite automata or Turing machines.

steps.title: Check statements C and D
steps.apply: C: NTM and DTM both recognize RE languages — equivalent. D: Multi-tape TM can be simulated by single-tape TM — equivalent. Both C and D are TRUE.

solution.result: B. Non-deterministic Push-down automata are equivalent to deterministic Push-down automata — this is FALSE.

questions.formula_note: NPDA strictly outpowers DPDA; NFA=DFA, NTM=DTM, multi-tape TM=single-tape TM are all true equivalences.

questions.question:
Let N_f and N_p denote the classes of languages accepted by non-deterministic finite automata and non-deterministic push-down automata, respectively. Let D_f and D_p denote the classes of languages accepted by deterministic finite automata and deterministic push-down automata respectively. Which one of the following is TRUE?
A. D_f subset N_f and D_p subset N_p
B. D_f = N_f and D_p = N_p
C. D_f = N_f and D_p subset N_p
D. D_f subset N_f and D_p = N_p
questions.answer: C. D_f = N_f and D_p subset N_p
questions.to_find: The correct relationship between language classes: D_f vs N_f and D_p vs N_p.

understand.plain: This question asks us to identify the correct set-theoretic relationship between the languages accepted by deterministic and non-deterministic versions of finite automata and pushdown automata. For finite automata, deterministic and non-deterministic versions accept exactly the same class (both = regular languages). For pushdown automata, DPDA is strictly weaker than NPDA (DCFL is a proper subset of CFL).

keywords.explain: The class of languages accepted by non-deterministic finite automata — exactly the regular languages.
keywords.example: N_f = {L | L is a regular language}.

keywords.explain: The class of languages accepted by deterministic pushdown automata — exactly the deterministic context-free languages (DCFL), a proper subset of CFL.
keywords.example: {a^n b^n} is in D_p; {ww^R} is in N_p but NOT in D_p.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="420" height="220" viewBox="0 0 420 220"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect class="bg" x="10" y="10" width="180" height="180" rx="10"/><rect class="hi" x="30" y="30" width="140" height="140" rx="8"/><text class="ink lbl" x="100" y="60" text-anchor="middle">N_f = D_f</text><text class="ink sub" x="100" y="80" text-anchor="middle">(Regular)</text><rect class="bg" x="230" y="10" width="180" height="180" rx="10"/><ellipse cx="310" cy="110" rx="80" ry="80" fill="none" stroke="#B4B2A9" stroke-width="2"/><ellipse class="hi" cx="310" cy="120" rx="50" ry="55"/><text class="ink lbl" x="310" y="115" text-anchor="middle">D_p</text><text class="ink sub" x="310" y="133" text-anchor="middle">(DCFL)</text><text class="ink sub" x="310" y="35" text-anchor="middle">N_p (CFL)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Left diagram: D_f equals N_f (same circle, both regular). Right diagram: D_p is a proper subset circle inside N_p (DCFL inside CFL).

given.aim: Determine whether D_f = N_f and/or D_p subset N_p or D_p = N_p.

terms.meaning: Deterministic and non-deterministic finite automata recognize exactly the same class of languages (regular languages).
terms.example: Subset construction converts any NFA to an equivalent DFA.
terms.connects: Eliminates options A (which claims D_f subset N_f, implying proper subset) and D.

terms.meaning: Deterministic PDA (DCFL) is strictly less powerful than non-deterministic PDA (CFL). Some CFLs have no equivalent DPDA.
terms.example: {ww^R} in N_p but not in D_p.
terms.connects: Rules out option B (D_p = N_p) and confirms C.

terms.meaning: If every DPDA language is accepted by some NPDA but not vice versa, then D_p is a proper subset of N_p.
terms.example: Every DPDA is also an NPDA (with no non-determinism), so D_p subset N_p. But N_p contains CFL not in D_p, so the subset is proper.

given.plan: D_f = N_f (regular language equivalence by subset construction). D_p subset N_p proper (DCFL is proper subset of CFL). This matches option C.

steps.title: Finite automata: D_f vs N_f
steps.apply: By the subset construction theorem, every NFA can be converted to an equivalent DFA. Therefore D_f = N_f. Note that D_f is NOT a proper subset of N_f — they are equal.
steps.note: This rules out options A and D which write D_f subset N_f (implying proper subset).

steps.title: Pushdown automata: D_p vs N_p
steps.formula_raw: D_p = DCFL subset CFL = N_p (proper subset)
steps.apply: NPDA recognizes all CFLs while DPDA recognizes only DCFLs. Since {ww^R} is a CFL not recognizable by any DPDA, we have D_p subset N_p (proper). They are NOT equal.
steps.note: This rules out option B which writes D_p = N_p.

steps.title: Select the correct option
steps.apply: D_f = N_f and D_p subset N_p (proper). This matches option C exactly.

solution.result: C. D_f = N_f and D_p subset N_p.

questions.formula_note: Subset construction gives D_f = N_f; DPDA recognizes only DCFL (proper subset of CFL recognized by NPDA), so D_p subset N_p strictly.

questions.question:
Which of the following pairs have DIFFERENT expressive power?
A. Deterministic finite automata (DFA) and Non-deterministic finite automata (NFA)
B. Deterministic push down automata (DPDA) and Non-deterministic push down automata (NPDA)
C. Deterministic single tape Turing machine and Non-deterministic single tape Turing machine
D. Single tape Turing machine and Multi-tape Turing machine
questions.answer: B. Deterministic push down automata (DPDA) and Non-deterministic push down automata (NPDA)
questions.to_find: Which pair of machine types has different expressive power (recognizes different classes of languages)?

understand.plain: The question asks which pair of machines does NOT have equivalent language recognition power. DFA and NFA are equivalent (both recognize regular languages). DTM and NTM are equivalent (both recognize RE languages). Single-tape and multi-tape TMs are equivalent. But DPDA and NPDA are NOT equivalent — NPDA recognizes all CFLs while DPDA only recognizes DCFLs, a proper subset.

keywords.explain: The class of languages a computational model can recognize. Two models have the same expressive power if they recognize exactly the same set of languages.
keywords.example: DFA and NFA have the same expressive power (both = regular languages), even though NFA is more succinct.

keywords.explain: DPDA recognizes deterministic context-free languages (DCFL); NPDA recognizes all context-free languages (CFL). Since CFL strictly contains DCFL, they have different expressive power.
keywords.example: The palindrome language {ww^R | w in {a,b}*} is in L(NPDA) but not in L(DPDA).

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="500" height="200" viewBox="0 0 500 200"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect class="ok" x="10" y="70" width="100" height="60" rx="8" opacity="0.3"/><text class="ink lbl" x="60" y="95" text-anchor="middle">DFA</text><text class="ink sub" x="60" y="115" text-anchor="middle">=NFA</text><rect class="no" x="130" y="40" width="110" height="120" rx="8" opacity="0.15"/><rect class="hi" x="140" y="80" width="90" height="40" rx="6"/><text class="ink lbl" x="185" y="97" text-anchor="middle">DPDA</text><text class="ink sub" x="185" y="145" text-anchor="middle">NPDA</text><text class="no tag" x="185" y="25" text-anchor="middle">DIFFERENT!</text><rect class="ok" x="260" y="70" width="100" height="60" rx="8" opacity="0.3"/><text class="ink lbl" x="310" y="95" text-anchor="middle">DTM</text><text class="ink sub" x="310" y="115" text-anchor="middle">=NTM</text><rect class="ok" x="380" y="70" width="110" height="60" rx="8" opacity="0.3"/><text class="ink lbl" x="435" y="95" text-anchor="middle">1-tape</text><text class="ink sub" x="435" y="115" text-anchor="middle">=multi-tape</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Four pairs: DFA=NFA (same power), DPDA inside NPDA (different power, DPDA is smaller), DTM=NTM (same power), single-tape TM = multi-tape TM (same power). Only DPDA vs NPDA is different.

given.aim: Identify the pair of automata/machines with genuinely different (non-equivalent) language recognition power.

terms.meaning: Both recognize exactly the regular languages. Subset construction converts NFA to DFA.
terms.example: NFA for (a+b)*ab -> DFA by subset construction.
terms.connects: Option A: SAME power — not the answer.

terms.meaning: DPDA recognizes DCFL (proper subset of CFL). NPDA recognizes all of CFL. They have DIFFERENT expressive power.
terms.example: {ww^R} is in CFL (NPDA-recognizable) but not in DCFL (no DPDA accepts it).
terms.connects: Option B: DIFFERENT power — this is the answer.

terms.meaning: Both recognize exactly the recursively enumerable (RE) languages. NTM can be simulated by DTM.
terms.example: DTM uses BFS to simulate NTM's branching computation.
terms.connects: Option C: SAME power — not the answer.

terms.meaning: Multi-tape TM can be simulated by single-tape TM by interleaving tape contents. Both recognize RE languages.
terms.example: Encode k tapes on 1 tape using delimiters and virtual head markers.
terms.connects: Option D: SAME power — not the answer.

given.plan: For each pair, recall whether they are equivalent in recognition power. Only DPDA vs NPDA differ (DCFL != CFL). The answer is B.

steps.title: Check pair A: DFA vs NFA
steps.apply: Subset construction theorem: every NFA can be converted to an equivalent DFA. Same expressive power. Option A does NOT have different expressive power.
steps.note: NFA may be exponentially more succinct but not more powerful.

steps.title: Check pair B: DPDA vs NPDA
steps.apply: NPDA recognizes all context-free languages. DPDA recognizes only deterministic context-free languages. Example: palindromes {ww^R} are in L(NPDA) but not in L(DPDA). They have DIFFERENT expressive power. Option B is the answer.
steps.note: This is the unique case among standard automata pairs where determinism reduces power.

steps.title: Check pair C: DTM vs NTM
steps.apply: BFS simulation by DTM covers all NTM computation paths. Same expressive power. Option C does NOT have different expressive power.
steps.note: NTM may be exponentially faster but not more powerful in terms of recognition.

steps.title: Check pair D: Single-tape TM vs Multi-tape TM
steps.formula_raw: Multi-tape TM can be simulated by single-tape TM
steps.apply: A single-tape TM can simulate a k-tape TM by encoding all tapes on one tape with delimiters. Both recognize RE languages. Same expressive power. Option D does NOT have different expressive power.
steps.note: Multi-tape TMs are more efficient (quadratic speedup) but not more powerful.

solution.result: B. Deterministic push down automata (DPDA) and Non-deterministic push down automata (NPDA) — they have different expressive power (DCFL vs CFL).

questions.formula_note: DPDA recognizes only DCFL (proper subset of CFL), while NPDA recognizes all CFLs; all other listed pairs have identical recognition power.
